pathophysiology_summary: Acute urticaria is mast-cell/basophil degranulation releasing histamine and other mediators, producing transient, migratory, intensely pruritic wheals with characteristic central pallor and surrounding flare; individual lesions resolve within 24 hours. It is frequently post-infectious (recent viral URI) and often has no identifiable allergen. The diagnosis itself is clinical and can be made confidently via video. The critical ground-truth task is continuous risk assessment for progression to angioedema/anaphylaxis (airway or cardiovascular involvement), which is absent here, and rational pharmacology: non-sedating H1-antihistamine, up-dosed to as much as 4× standard dose before adding other agents, with a short oral corticosteroid course for severe refractory flares.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden widespread migratory pruritic wheals with central pallor, individual lesions transient — textbook urticaria.
Urticaria can be the cutaneous prodrome of anaphylaxis; must actively exclude airway/CVS/GI involvement from the first turn.
Considered but argued against by the migratory, transient (<24h) nature and absence of fixed target lesions.
Generalised migratory distribution rather than localised to an exposure site argues against contact dermatitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Urticaria may lead but anaphylaxis must be listed important_not_to_miss and actively excluded before any reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No mucosal/airway swelling, no CVS/GI features — anaphylaxis excluded on current history. Recent viral URI supports post-viral acute urticaria.
No airway, cardiovascular or GI involvement — anaphylaxis criteria not met. Remains the escalation target if features develop.
Transient migratory wheals, no fixed target lesions.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative anaphylaxis screen, isolated acute urticaria leads; management is antihistamine up-dosing with a hard anaphylaxis safety-net, not emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting. No investigations indicated for a first acute episode.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is isolated acute urticaria for home management with safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Angioedema / airway involvement — lip, tongue or throat swelling, voice change, stridor, difficulty breathing/swallowing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks progression from urticaria to anaphylaxis/angioedema — a life-threatening airway emergency requiring adrenaline and 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised evolving airway angioedema leads to asphyxiation.

red_flags.description: Cardiovascular/GI features of anaphylaxis — dizziness, faintness, hypotension, vomiting, abdominal pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic anaphylaxis with two or more organ systems requires immediate adrenaline.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Responding to failure of one standard-dose 2nd-gen antihistamine by switching to a sedating 1st-gen agent instead of up-dosing the non-sedating antihistamine (to 2–4× standard dose), the guideline-preferred step.
Sending an isolated urticaria with a fully negative anaphylaxis screen to ED by ambulance — over-uses resources; correct is home management with a clear anaphylaxis safety-net.
Reassuring before excluding airway/CVS/GI involvement — urticaria can be the prodrome of anaphylaxis.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the chest for wheeze
Direct examination of the oral mucosa/uvula for early angioedema
Blood pressure measurement to detect early hypotension
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Home management with antihistamine up-dosing; call 000 immediately if any airway/CVS/GI features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring without an airway/CVS/GI screen risks missing early anaphylaxis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Second-generation (non-sedating) H1-antihistamines are first-line and may be up-dosed to 4× standard dose; a short oral corticosteroid course may help severe flares. Provide an anaphylaxis action plan / safety-net.
Most acute urticaria is self-limiting and often post-viral; investigations are not required for a single acute episode without systemic features.
